elements.sent-2411-001: We show that a domain in $\mathbb{R ^3$ with the trace of a 3D Brownian motion removed almost surely satisfies the boundary Harnack principle (BHP).}
elements.sent-2411-002: Then, we use it to prove that the intersection exponents for 3D Brownian motion are analytic.
elements.sent-2411-003: Key words and phrases: Brownian motion, analyticity, intersection exponent, boundary Harnack principle, twisted H\"older domain, separation.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we prove that the intersection exponents for 3D Brownian motions are analytic.
elements.sent-2411-005: This property has been shown to hold in 2D by Lawler, Schramm and Werner in \cite{LSW02a .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Due to the absence of a rich conformal symmetry in 3D, the strategy used in the 2D case cannot be applied directly, and thus making the proof of analyticity in 3D a long-standing open problem.
elements.sent-2411-007: By analyzing the fine geometric structure of the random slit domain in $\Rb^3$, whose boundary is given by the trace of a Brownian motion, we establish a conditional version of separation lemma.
elements.sent-2411-008: As we will show in this paper, it is indeed equivalent to a boundary Harnack principle (BHP) for the slit domain.
elements.sent-2411-009: Surprisingly, this BHP in 3D serves as an effective substitute for the conformal invariance in 2D to derive the analyticity of Brownian intersection exponents.
elements.sent-2411-010: Section~\ref{subsec:analyticity is devoted to the result of analyticity of Brownian intersection exponents.}

elements.sent-2411-014: We begin by reviewing the Brownian intersection exponents in $\Rb^d$, $d=2,3$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Let $k$ be a positive integer, and let $W_1, \dots, W_k$ denote $k$ independent Brownian motions, each started at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, let $W_0$ be another independent Brownian motion, this time started at $(1,0)$ in 2D or $(1,0,0)$ in 3D.
elements.sent-2411-017: We use $\Pb$ and $\Eb$ to denote the respective probability measure and expectation for these processes.
elements.sent-2411-018: Let $\Bc_n$ be the ball of radius $e^n$ about $0$, and $\tau^i_n$ be the first hitting time of $\partial\Bc_n$ by $W_i$ for all $0\le i\le k$.
elements.sent-2411-019: For $n\ge 0$, consider the following conditional probability \begin{equation}Z_n:=\Pb\Big(W_0[0,\tau^0_n]\cap \big(W_1[0,\tau^1_n]\cup\ldots\cup W_k[0,\tau^k_n]\big)=\varnothing \ \Big| \ W_1[0,\tau^1_n],\ldots,W_k[0,\tau^k_n]\Big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: For all real $\lambda>0$, the intersection exponent in $d=2,3$ is defined by \begin{equation}\label{eq:exponent} \xi_d(k,\lambda):=-\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\frac{\log \Eb[Z_n^\lambda]}{n},\end{equation} where the existence of this limit follows from a standard subadditivity argument.
elements.sent-2411-021: The intersection exponents are closely linked to other critical exponents that arise in statistical physics, such as those in critical planar percolation and self-avoiding walks (see \cite{MR1796962, MR1905353, LSW01a, LSW02a, MR2112127 for detailed discussions).}
elements.sent-2411-022: In particular, the values of $\xi_2(k,\lambda)$ were originally predicted by Duplantier and Kwon \cite{DK88 using methods from conformal field theory.}

elements.sent-2411-024: Through the connection between planar Brownian motion and Schramm-Loewner evolution (SLE$_6$), along with the conformal invariance of Brownian motion, Lawler, Schramm and Werner provided a rigorous proof of this conjecture in a series of celebrated works \cite{LSW01a,LSW01b,LSW02,LSW02a .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In particular, in \cite{LSW02a , they established that $\xi_2(k,\lambda)$ is a real analytic function of $\lambda$ on the interval $(0, \infty)$, a result which plays a crucial role in determining the values of these exponents across the entire range of $\lambda$.}

elements.sent-2411-056: For completeness of exposition, in the following, we briefly summarize the development of classical (non-conditional) separation lemmas.
elements.sent-2411-057: Broadly speaking, the separation lemma asserts that if two random processes are conditioned not to intersect, then they have a positive probability of being ``well-separated'' at their endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-058: This separation phenomenon has been observed and rigorously established in various statistical physics models, where it is instrumental in deriving quasi-multiplicativity properties of certain probabilities and in determining the Hausdorff dimension of specific random fractals.
elements.sent-2411-059: Given the extensive literature on this topic, we provide only a selective overview of separation lemmas across different models.
elements.sent-2411-060: This type of separation result was first employed by Kesten \cite{Ke1987a in studying arm events in two-dimensional percolation (see, e.g., \cite{BN2021,SS2010,DS11 for subsequent alternative proofs and generalizations).}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In a distinct context, Lawler \cite{Law96,Law96a,G98 established the separation lemma for non-intersecting Brownian motions, which served as a key ingredient for determining the Hausdorff dimension of cut points and the Brownian frontier.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Lawler's approach was later streamlined and applied to show strong separation properties for other models in two and three dimensions, including random walks \cite{RWcuttimes , loop-erased random walks \cite{Ma2009,LERW3exp,La2020 , and Brownian loop soups \cite{GLQ2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: A unified approach that synthesizes ideas from Kesten and Lawler was presented in the appendix of \cite{GPS2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Recently, this refined approach has been adapted to prove stronger versions of separation lemmas in critical planar percolation \cite{du2022sharp , as well as variations for random walks within a random walk loop soup \cite{GNQ2024a .}}

elements.section-2411-009: Downward deviation of cover times in a general setting
